region_of_origin: United States (Angus breed origin); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Beef — grilled red meat, primary cut

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef ribeye steak entry; the grain-fed Angus specification and 250g portion are not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers grilled beef meat broadly; no specific Angus grain-fed ribeye code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226847004 covers beef steak generically; no specific ribeye or Angus sub-code available.

allergens: none identified; may contain traces depending on kitchen cross-contact

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a 250g char-grilled Angus beef ribeye steak, served with beef jus and roasted garlic, as plated at the restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, rib eye steak, boneless, lip-on, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8" fat, all grades, cooked, grilled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #23340
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for grilled beef ribeye (boneless, lean and fat, all grades) used as the closest approved match for a grain-fed Angus ribeye. Grain-fed Angus ribeye is nutritionally similar to USDA all-grades grilled ribeye; minor differences in fat composition may exist. Values are per 100g cooked weight. Serving-level values scaled to 250g. Beef jus and roasted garlic contribute negligible macronutrients at typical serving volumes and are not separately itemised.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database — beef and meat entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure beef (no carbohydrate-containing coating or marinade) has a glycemic index of effectively zero because it contains no digestible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature consistently assign GI = 0 for plain cooked beef. Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule (q4), glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (1 × 0.0 × 250) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content per 100g (65 mg); moderate saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: A 250g serving provides approximately 163 mg sodium, well within the 2300 mg daily limit; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total saturated fat intake (approximately 21g per serving) and request low-sodium beef jus to minimise added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero digestible carbohydrates; no glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled beef ribeye contains no digestible carbohydrates and has a glycemic index of 1, making it suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; portion size should be moderated due to high saturated fat content, which may contribute to insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (8.5g/100g) and cholesterol (87mg/100g); a 250g serving delivers approximately 21g saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish to occasional meals, as a 250g serving provides approximately 21g saturated fat — exceeding the AHA recommended daily limit of 13g — and 218mg dietary cholesterol; choosing leaner cuts or smaller portions is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char-Grilled Grain-Fed Angus Beef Ribeye 250g is a premium Western steakhouse dish served at Mingle - Funan in Singapore. The ribeye is sourced from grain-fed Angus cattle, a breed prized for its superior marbling, tenderness, and rich beefy flavour. The steak is char-grilled over high heat, producing a caramelised crust (Maillard reaction) while retaining a juicy, tender interior. It is served with beef jus — a concentrated reduction of beef cooking juices — and roasted garlic, which adds sweetness and depth. The 250g portion is a substantial restaurant serving. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 62g per serving) and fat (approximately 52g per serving), with zero carbohydrates, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. The saturated fat content is high (approximately 21g per serving), which is a concern for cardiovascular disease and should be consumed in moderation. It has a very low glycemic impact (GI=1) due to the absence of digestible carbohydrates, making it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed in controlled portions. The dish is rich in zinc, vitamin B12, iron, and selenium — nutrients important for immune function, red blood cell production, and antioxidant defence. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore, this dish is typically found in Western casual-dining and steakhouse restaurants and is priced at the premium end of the market.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a 250g ribeye steak?
Is ribeye steak good for diabetes?
Protein content of grilled Angus beef ribeye
Is ribeye steak high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy grilled beef ribeye (all grades), which is the closest approved source. Grain-fed Angus ribeye is nutritionally comparable but may have slightly higher monounsaturated fat due to grain feeding. No Singapore-specific or Angus-specific approved source entry was available. Confidence is medium rather than high due to this adaptation.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain grilled beef ribeye — confirmed zero. 2. sugar_g is confirmed zero — confirmed zero. 3. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for pure beef — confirmed zero. 4. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero for cooked beef — confirmed zero. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero for beef ribeye per USDA — confirmed zero. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this cut. 7. glycemic_index is set to 1 because plain grilled beef contains no digestible carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g = 0. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.9g/100g reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and vaccenic acid in beef, not industrial trans fats; this is per USDA data. 9. The beef jus and roasted garlic accompaniments contribute negligible macronutrients at typical serving volumes and are not separately itemised in the per_100g values. 10. Halal status is not confirmed for this restaurant; is_halal set to false pending verification. 11. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Italian / Pizza / Pasta' which is inconsistent with a beef ribeye steakhouse dish; cuisine has been corrected to 'Western / Steakhouse' based on the dish identity.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Italian/Pizza/Pasta' (input error) to 'Western/Steakhouse' based on dish identity.
Added multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 for Singapore-served dish.
Populated full per_100g nutrient profile from USDA SR Legacy grilled beef ribeye.
Documented all confirmed-zero values (carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae) with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value with explicit reason in notes (revised for clarity per G3 feedback).
Set glycemic_index=1 (from 0) per Q4 rule for effectively zero-GI foods, updated gi_source notes accordingly. gi_category='low' and glycemic_load_per_serving=0.0 remain correct.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Built comprehensive aliases_by_language and verified consistency with flat aliases list.
Populated llm_training section with >200 character description, user queries, meal context, and ingredient synonyms.
Applied correct ontology match_type values ('closest_parent') for all three ontology systems.
